description: The figure presents a line chart showing the evolution of phase volume fractions as a function of annealing temperature for Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel, determined from XRD measurements. The x-axis spans from the hot-rolled (HR) state through annealing temperatures of 700 °C to 1200 °C, while the y-axis indicates phase volume fraction from 0 to 1.0. Three curves are plotted: α-ferrite (red squares), γ-austenite (blue triangles), and κ-carbide (orange stars). The α phase peaks at approximately 0.84 at 700 °C, then decreases sharply to about 0.40 at 900 °C, after which it remains relatively stable between 0.37 and 0.43 up to 1200 °C. The γ phase is absent at 700 °C, increases monotonically with temperature to reach roughly 0.60 at 900 °C, peaks near 0.63 at 1000 °C, and then gradually declines to about 0.57 at 1200 °C. The κ-carbide fraction decreases continuously from approximately 0.16 at 700 °C to near zero between 850 °C and 900 °C; the caption notes it cannot be detected by XRD at 900 °C, indicating complete dissolution. These trends confirm that κ-carbide dissolution occurs between 850 °C and 900 °C, higher than the 823 °C predicted by Thermo-Calc, and that the steel enters a stable α + γ duplex regime above 900 °C with only minor phase fraction variations, providing a microstructural basis for interpreting subsequent tensile property changes.
